title: "An endless demand for compute" | Jonathan Ross, founder of Groq
summary: Jonathan Ross founded Groq, a specialized hardware chip for LLM inference. Groq entered a 20 billion dollar strategic agreement with NVIDIA. The discussion covers LPU vs GPU tradeoffs, static scheduling, mixture-of-experts models, and inference optimization techniques.
writeup: Julia Turc interviews Jonathan Ross, founder of Groq, about the company's LPU, a specialized chip built for LLM inference, and a 20 billion dollar strategic agreement with NVIDIA. The conversation contrasts LPU and GPU tradeoffs, explains static scheduling, and covers how mixture-of-experts models interact with inference optimization. It matters because hardware design choices shape the cost and capacity behind growing token demand. Key takeaways: static scheduling distinguishes the LPU approach, mixture-of-experts factors into inference optimization, and demand for compute remains the framing constraint.
